blurb: The bulletproof spring bulb — golden trumpets that come back and multiply for decades, and that deer, rabbits, and voles won't touch because every part is poisonous.
water: Low; water in spring growth, then dry summer dormancy
spread: Naturalizing; multiplies by offsets into ever-larger long-lived clumps
seasons: Yellow-to-white trumpet flowers late winter through late spring (Mar–Apr here); strappy leaves spring; fully dormant by midsummer; nothing above ground fall–winter
wildlife: Early-spring nectar and pollen for bees when in bloom; otherwise low wildlife value
deer: Excellent — deer-, rabbit-, and rodent-proof; all parts are toxic so animals leave the bulbs and flowers alone (often interplanted to protect tulips)
toxic: Toxic — all parts contain phenanthridine alkaloids (chiefly lycorine) plus calcium oxalate crystals, the bulb most poisonous; ingestion causes nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, salivation, and trembling in people and pets, and handling can cause 'lily rash' contact dermatitis; bulbs are dangerously mistaken for onions

care.hardiness: USDA zones 4a–8b — fully hardy in Boulder's zone 5b–6a and one of the most reliable, longest-lived spring bulbs for the Front Range; established clumps need no winter protection and return for decades, multiplying as they go.[1]
care.planting: Plant bulbs in fall, mid-September through late October (early autumn, ideally September), before the soil freezes, so they root over winter.[2][3] Set them pointed-end up about 3 times the bulb's height deep — roughly 6–8 in. for standard bulbs — and 3–6 in. apart.[1][2] Boulder's clay drains slowly; work grit into the planting pocket and avoid spots where water sits, since waterlogged soil rots the bulbs.[1] Choose a site that bakes dry in summer. In containers, use a deep, fast-draining pot and overwinter it in an unheated garage or cold frame to buffer freeze-thaw; potted bulbs are far less cold-protected than in-ground ones.[2]
care.sun: Full sun to part shade — 6+ hours gives the heaviest bloom, but they take a few hours of sun and naturalize well under deciduous trees that are bare while the daffodils flower.[1]
care.soil: Well-drained soil; daffodils tolerate clay, loam, sand, and organic-rich ground but rot in soggy spots.[1] They lean slightly acidic by preference, yet naturalize dependably in Boulder's alkaline clay — focus on drainage rather than pH and amend heavy holes with grit.[1][2]
care.water: Low. Water in at fall planting and again if fall is dry. During spring growth and bloom, snowmelt and rain are usually enough; supplement only in a warm dry spell. After foliage yellows in early summer, stop watering and let the soil go dry through dormancy — summer moisture risks bulb rot.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space bulbs about 3–6 in. apart (10–12 bulbs per square foot for a dense drift); cluster them in generous groups rather than single file for the best naturalized look.[1]
care.propagation: By offsets (division) — the main method: established bulbs split into daughter bulbs and form ever-larger clumps; when a congested clump flowers less, lift it in summer dormancy, separate the offsets, and replant immediately at depth in new spots.[3] By seed: possible but slow (often 5+ years to first bloom) and seedlings of named cultivars won't come true, so division is the practical route; let species daffodils self-sow if you want a wild drift.[1][3]
care.depth: Plant bulbs about 3 times their height deep — roughly 6–8 in. for standard bulbs, deeper in light sandy soil.[1][2][3]
care.bloom: Late winter through late spring depending on type; on the Front Range most bloom March into April, each flower lasting two to three weeks. Mixing early, mid, and late cultivars stretches the season.[1]
care.feeding: Optional. A low-nitrogen bulb fertilizer or bone meal at fall planting and a light feed as shoots emerge supports the bulb; avoid high-nitrogen feeds, which push leaves over flowers.[1]
care.maintenance: Let the foliage die back fully (yellow) before removing it — green leaves recharge the bulb for next year; do not cut, fold, or braid it. Deadhead spent flowers so the plant doesn't waste energy on seed. Lift and divide clumps that have grown crowded and bloom-shy, every several years, during summer dormancy.[1][3]
care.troubles: Bulb rot in wet, poorly drained, or summer-watered soil is the main failure.[1] Narcissus bulb fly larvae, bulb mites, slugs, and snails can affect bulbs and foliage; basal rot shows in storage.[1] A real plus on the Front Range: because every part is toxic, deer, rabbits, voles, and squirrels leave daffodils alone, so they rarely suffer the browsing and bulb-digging that plague tulips.[1]
care.companions: Interplant with tulips and crocus, whose bulbs the daffodils' deer/rodent deterrence helps protect, and with later-rising perennials and groundcovers (catmint, hardy geranium, daylily) that hide the daffodils' fading foliage as it dies back.[1]

edible.summary: Do not eat any part of a daffodil — every part is poisonous, the bulb most of all, and bulbs are dangerously mistaken for onions.[1][4]
edible.caution: All parts of Narcissus contain phenanthridine alkaloids — chiefly lycorine — plus calcium oxalate crystals, with the highest concentration in the bulb.[1][4] Ingestion causes nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, hypersalivation, and trembling, and in larger amounts convulsions, cardiac effects, and it may be fatal; it is toxic to people, dogs, cats, and horses.[1][4] The most common serious poisonings happen when daffodil bulbs are mistaken for onions and eaten — never cook or eat an unlabeled bulb. Handling bulbs and stems can cause contact dermatitis ('lily rash' or 'daffodil itch') and the cut-stem sap can irritate skin; wear gloves. Keep bulbs and plants away from children and pets, and if ingestion is suspected call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) or the ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center (888-426-4435).[1][4]
